title: Tool Setup Guide: Java, Git, and GitHub Copilot
text:
This guide helps you get the required tools installed and opened for Week 1. After completing these checks, use Assignment 1 to practice with the tools, create your repository, capture screenshots, and complete the reflection.
The tool order below matches Assignment 1: JDK, IDE, Git/GitHub, Copilot, then a simple Java project.

title: Tool Setup Guide: Java, Git, and GitHub Copilot
text:
1. Install JDK 17+
Install a JDK from one of these sources:

Adoptium Temurin
Oracle Java Downloads

Confirm Java is available from a terminal:
java -version
You should see version 17 or newer.

title: Tool Setup Guide: Java, Git, and GitHub Copilot
text:
2. Install One IDE
Choose one development environment for the course. You do not need both.
Option A: IntelliJ IDEA

Download IntelliJ IDEA from JetBrains.
JetBrains recently merged the Community Edition into the main IntelliJ IDEA product offering. Students can also check the JetBrains Student Pack for access options.
Open IntelliJ and confirm it can create or open a Java project.

Option B: Visual Studio Code

Download VS Code from code.visualstudio.com.
Open Extensions (Cmd+Shift+X on macOS or Ctrl+Shift+X on Windows/Linux).
Install Extension Pack for Java.

Maven, Gradle, and JUnit are useful later in the course, but they are not required for the basic Assignment 1 setup check.

title: Tool Setup Guide: Java, Git, and GitHub Copilot
text:
3. Set Up Git and GitHub
Assignment 1 asks you to submit a Git repository link, so make sure Git and GitHub are ready before you start the assignment work.

Install Git from git-scm.com if it is not already installed.
Confirm Git is available:

git --version

Sign in to GitHub or create an account.
Set up GitHub Education using your university email address at github.com/settings/education/benefits. This is how students access GitHub Education benefits, including Copilot access when approved.
Confirm you know how you will connect to GitHub: HTTPS or SSH. GitHub’s docs explain both options in Set up Git.

title: Tool Setup Guide: Java, Git, and GitHub Copilot
text:
4. Enable GitHub Copilot
GitHub Copilot is the only AI assistant approved for Assignment 1. Before installing the IDE plugin, request or confirm GitHub Education benefits with your university email address at github.com/settings/education/benefits.
In IntelliJ IDEA

Open Settings/Preferences > Plugins.
Search for GitHub Copilot in the Marketplace and install it.
Restart IntelliJ if prompted.
Sign in with your GitHub account when prompted.

In VS Code

Open Extensions.
Install GitHub Copilot.
Sign in with your GitHub account when prompted.

If you need access help, see GitHub Copilot documentation.

title: Tool Setup Guide: Java, Git, and GitHub Copilot
Tool Setup Guide: Java, Git, and GitHub Copilot
text:
6. Confirm Copilot Suggestions Appear
In Main.java, type a short comment such as:
// method to add two numbers
Pause briefly and look for Copilot ghost text. If a suggestion appears, accept it with Tab or your IDE’s configured accept-suggestion shortcut.
Do not spend time making this code complex. The goal is only to confirm that Java, your IDE, GitHub Copilot, and GitHub are ready.

title: Tool Setup Guide: Java, Git, and GitHub Copilot
text:
7. Continue in Assignment 1
This setup guide stops once the tools are working. Complete the actual Week 1 deliverables in Assignment 1: Environment Setup & AI-Assisted SWE Foundations.
Assignment 1 is where you will:

Create the Git repository.
Commit the minimal Java project.
Add one Copilot-generated method.
Add a README.md with setup confirmation.
Capture screenshots.
Complete the conceptual reflection.

title: Tool Setup Guide: Java, Git, and GitHub Copilot
text:
Need Help?

Java in VS Code
IntelliJ IDEA documentation
GitHub Docs: Set up Git
GitHub Copilot documentation
Ask questions in your course forum or contact your instructor.

title: Final Project Milestone 1: Proposal & Architecture
text:
Course Name/Number: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
Week: 10
Points: 80
This milestone builds on Labs 1-5, especially Lab 5: Coding Agents and Controlled Workflows.

title: Final Project Milestone 1: Proposal & Architecture
text:
1 Purpose of Assignment
This assignment is intended to help you:

Define the scope of your Controlled Coding Agent
Design a clear system architecture based on Labs 1-5
Establish a structured AI-assisted workflow
Create a realistic development plan for completion

title: Final Project Milestone 1: Proposal & Architecture
text:
2 Steps / Requirements of Assignment
Prepare a proposal and architecture plan for your final Controlled Coding Agent system. You may continue your lab repository or create a dedicated final project repository.

Define the problem your coding agent addresses.
Describe intended users and expected workflow.
Create an architecture diagram or structured architecture description.
Identify core components, including input, generation, evaluation, workflow, and persistence.
Create a development plan for Weeks 10-12.

title: Final Project Milestone 1: Proposal & Architecture
text:
3 Action Items

Create or update your project repository.
Add documentation for proposal and architecture.
Begin implementation if possible.

title: Final Project Milestone 1: Proposal & Architecture
text:
4 Deliverables
Your repository should include:

Project proposal.
Architecture documentation or diagram.
Development plan and milestone checklist.
Initial implementation artifacts if available.

title: Final Project Milestone 1: Proposal & Architecture
text:
5 Grading



Category
Points




Project scope and problem statement are clear
20


Architecture covers major system components
25


Structured AI-assisted workflow is defined
15


Development plan is realistic and specific
15


Repository organization is ready for continued work
5


Total
80

title: Final Project Milestone 1: Proposal & Architecture
text:
6 Submission Instructions
Submit your project repository link through Canvas. Include links or paths to the proposal and architecture documentation.
Next: Final Project Milestone 2.

title: Lab 4: AI Risk Analysis and Production Readiness
text:
Course Name/Number: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
Week: 9
Points: 90
This lab analyzes the system built in Lab 3: AI System Implementation. Continue maintaining the Weekly AI Interaction Log.

title: Lab 4: AI Risk Analysis and Production Readiness
text:
1 Purpose of Assignment
This assignment is intended to help you:

Analyze failure modes in your coding agent
Evaluate reliability, security, and maintainability
Propose improvements to increase robustness

title: Lab 4: AI Risk Analysis and Production Readiness
text:
2 Steps / Requirements of Assignment
Conduct a risk analysis of your current coding agent workflow. Focus on realistic failures and production-readiness concerns rather than generic AI risks.

Identify at least five failure modes in your system.
Analyze reliability, security, maintainability, and human oversight risks.
Rate each risk by likelihood and impact.
Propose concrete mitigations.
Identify which mitigations you can implement before the final project submission.

title: Lab 4: AI Risk Analysis and Production Readiness
text:
3 Action Items

Analyze your system.
Document findings clearly.
Add risk analysis documentation to your repository.
Record any AI-assisted analysis interactions in your weekly log.

title: Lab 4: AI Risk Analysis and Production Readiness
text:
4 Deliverables
Your repository should include:

Risk analysis document or README section.
Failure mode table with likelihood, impact, and mitigation.
Production-readiness checklist.
Prioritized improvement plan for the final project.

title: Lab 4: AI Risk Analysis and Production Readiness
text:
5 Grading







Category
Points




Failure modes are specific to the student’s system
25


Reliability, security, maintainability, and oversight are addressed
25


Risk ratings and mitigations are concrete
20


Production-readiness plan is actionable
15


AI interaction evidence is included
5


Total
90

title: Lab 4: AI Risk Analysis and Production Readiness
text:
6 Submission Instructions
Submit your repository link and risk analysis document through Canvas.
Next lab: Lab 5: Coding Agents and Controlled Workflows.

title: Final Project Final Submission: Complete System, Documentation, Evaluation Report, and Demo
text:
Course Name/Number: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
Week: 12
Points: 180
This final submission completes the project developed through Final Project Milestone 1 and Final Project Milestone 2.

title: Final Project Final Submission: Complete System, Documentation, Evaluation Report, and Demo
text:
1 Purpose of Assignment
This assignment is intended to help you:

Deliver a complete Controlled Coding Agent system
Demonstrate integration of all course components
Apply engineering judgment to AI-assisted workflows
Clearly communicate system design and results

title: Final Project Final Submission: Complete System, Documentation, Evaluation Report, and Demo
text:
2 Steps / Requirements of Assignment
Finalize your Controlled Coding Agent system and submit a complete, reproducible project. Your final submission should demonstrate integration of structured input, AI-assisted generation, evaluation, controlled workflow, persistence or artifact tracking, documentation, and human oversight.

title: Final Project Final Submission: Complete System, Documentation, Evaluation Report, and Demo
text:
3 Action Items

Finalize and verify your complete coding agent system, including input, generation, evaluation, workflow, and persistence.
Ensure the project runs from a clean environment with clear setup instructions.
Provide a reproducible demo command or script that executes the full workflow.
Submit a 5-8 minute recorded demo showing system functionality and iteration.
Include an example workflow run and evaluation results in your README.
Provide evidence of AI-assisted development, including prompts, iterations, and Copilot usage.
Review submission for completeness, clarity, and usability.

title: Final Project Final Submission: Complete System, Documentation, Evaluation Report, and Demo
text:
4 Deliverables
Your repository should include:

Complete runnable Controlled Coding Agent system.
README with setup, run, evaluation, and demo instructions.
Evaluation report with results and interpretation.
Example workflow run artifacts.
AI-assisted development evidence.
Link to a 5-8 minute recorded demo.

title: Final Project Final Submission: Complete System, Documentation, Evaluation Report, and Demo
text:
5 Grading







Category
Points




Complete system integrates input, generation, evaluation, workflow, and persistence/artifact tracking
50


System runs from a clean environment with clear instructions
25


Evaluation report is meaningful and evidence-based
35


Documentation clearly explains architecture, usage, and limitations
30


Demo is clear, complete, and within 5-8 minutes
25


AI-assisted development evidence is complete and transparent
15


Total
180

title: Final Project Final Submission: Complete System, Documentation, Evaluation Report, and Demo
text:
6 Submission Instructions
Submit your project repository link and recorded demo link through Canvas. Follow any additional sharing instructions provided by your professor.

title: Lab 5: Coding Agents and Controlled Workflows
text:
Course Name/Number: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
Week: 10
Points: 100
This lab extends Lab 4: AI Risk Analysis and Production Readiness and prepares you for Final Project Milestone 1.

title: Lab 5: Coding Agents and Controlled Workflows
text:
1 Purpose of Assignment
This assignment is intended to help you:

Implement the core agent loop
Design controlled multi-step workflows
Introduce human oversight into your coding agent
Move from simple workflows to agent-like behavior

title: Lab 5: Coding Agents and Controlled Workflows
text:
2 Steps / Requirements of Assignment
Implement a controlled multi-step workflow that resembles an agent loop while keeping human oversight explicit. The system should not blindly generate and apply changes without review.

Define the loop stages, such as intake, plan, generate, evaluate, revise, and approve.
Implement the workflow in code, scripts, or a clearly runnable process.
Add at least one human approval checkpoint.
Preserve artifacts from a workflow run, including input, generated output, evaluation result, and final decision.
Document how to reproduce the workflow.

title: Lab 5: Coding Agents and Controlled Workflows
text:
3 Action Items

Implement the workflow in your repository.
Document how the workflow operates.
Ensure reproducibility.
Record relevant AI interactions in your final Week 10 AI interaction log entry.

title: Lab 5: Coding Agents and Controlled Workflows
text:
4 Deliverables
Your repository should include:

Runnable controlled workflow.
Documentation of the agent loop stages.
Human oversight checkpoint description.
Example run artifacts.
Reproducible setup and execution instructions.

title: Lab 5: Coding Agents and Controlled Workflows
text:
5 Grading



Category
Points




Core agent loop is implemented and understandable
30


Multi-step workflow is controlled and reproducible
25


Human oversight checkpoint is meaningful
20


Example run artifacts are complete
15


Documentation is clear and usable
10


Total
100

title: Lab 5: Coding Agents and Controlled Workflows
text:
6 Submission Instructions
Submit your repository link through Canvas. Clearly identify the command, script, or steps your professor should use to reproduce the workflow.
Next: Final Project Milestone 1: Proposal & Architecture.

title: Assignment 1: Environment Setup & AI-Assisted SWE Foundations
text:
Course Name/Number: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
Week: 1
Points: 30
Use the Tool Setup Guide to install and start the required tools before completing this assignment.

title: Assignment 1: Environment Setup & AI-Assisted SWE Foundations
text:
1 Purpose of Assignment
This assignment is intended to help you:

Set up your development environment for AI-assisted software engineering
Verify GitHub Copilot is working correctly
Understand the difference between vibe coding and structured AI-assisted development
Introduce the concept of programming with intent
Recognize key strengths and limitations of AI-generated code
Interpret the Software 2.0 perspective

title: Assignment 1: Environment Setup & AI-Assisted SWE Foundations
text:
2 Steps / Requirements of Assignment
Complete the following setup tasks:

Install JDK 17+ and verify installation with:

java -version

Install one development environment:

IntelliJ IDEA, or
VS Code with Java extensions

Install Git, sign in to GitHub, set up GitHub Education using your university email address at github.com/settings/education/benefits, and confirm you can create or access a GitHub repository.
Install and sign in to GitHub Copilot.
Create a simple Java project named:

ai-swe-course

Add a basic Main.java file.
Verify Copilot suggestions are working:

Type a simple comment such as:


// method to add two numbers

Confirm Copilot ghost text appears.
Accept at least one suggestion.


Generate one simple method using Copilot.

Examples: - Add two numbers - Reverse a string - Check if a number is even
Keep the code simple. The goal is to confirm your setup works.

title: Assignment 1: Environment Setup & AI-Assisted SWE Foundations
text:
3 Conceptual Reflection
Write a short reflection of 150–250 words addressing the following:

What is the difference between vibe coding and AI-assisted software engineering?
What does programming with intent mean in practice?
What are key strengths and limitations of AI-generated code?
How do you interpret the Software 2.0 perspective, and what are its implications for software development?

Your response should be concise, thoughtful, and written in your own words.

title: Assignment 1: Environment Setup & AI-Assisted SWE Foundations
text:
4 Action Items

Create a Git repository
Commit a minimal Java project
Add one Copilot-generated method
Add a README.md with setup confirmation
Capture and include screenshots
Complete the short reflection

title: Assignment 1: Environment Setup & AI-Assisted SWE Foundations
text:
5 Deliverables
Submit the following:

5.1 1. Git Repository
Your repository should include:

A minimal Java project
At least one Copilot-generated method
A README.md that includes:

Setup confirmation
One sentence stating that Copilot is working




5.2 2. Screenshots
Include 2–3 screenshots showing:

Your IDE with the Java project open
A Copilot suggestion visible as ghost text
Optional: Copilot settings page



5.3 3. Reflection
Submit your 150–250 word conceptual reflection.

title: Assignment 1: Environment Setup & AI-Assisted SWE Foundations
text:
6 Grading
Total: 30 points



Category
Points




Environment, GitHub, and Copilot setup completed
10


Screenshots clearly demonstrate setup
5


Repository includes minimal Java project and README
5


Reflection demonstrates understanding of Week 1 concepts
10


Total
30

title: Assignment 1: Environment Setup & AI-Assisted SWE Foundations
text:
7 Submission Instructions

Submit your Git repository link.
Submit your screenshots and reflection through Canvas.
Follow any additional sharing instructions provided by your professor.

title: Assignment 1: Environment Setup & AI-Assisted SWE Foundations
text:
8 Notes

Only GitHub Copilot is approved for this assignment.
Do not over-engineer the code portion.
If you encounter setup issues, briefly document what happened and how you attempted to resolve it.
Deeper prompting and structured AI-assisted development will begin in later assignments.

title: Lecture 9: Ethical, Legal, and Societal Implications of AI-Assisted Software Engineering
text:
Overview
This lecture explores the ethical, legal, and societal challenges that arise when integrating AI-powered coding assistants into software engineering workflows.
Key Topics

Bias in AI-generated code and its impact on fairness
Intellectual property and copyright concerns
Accountability and transparency in AI-assisted development
Societal impacts: workforce, trust, and responsibility

title: Lecture 9: Ethical, Legal, and Societal Implications of AI-Assisted Software Engineering
text:
Learning Objectives

Evaluate ethical issues in AI-generated code
Discuss intellectual property and accountability
Analyze societal impacts of AI adoption in software engineering

title: Lecture 9: Ethical, Legal, and Societal Implications of AI-Assisted Software Engineering
text:
Discussion Prompt
Reflect on a scenario where AI-generated code introduces bias or copyright issues. How should developers and organizations respond?

title: Lecture 9: Ethical, Legal, and Societal Implications of AI-Assisted Software Engineering
text:
Activity: Ethical Analysis

Review a sample AI-generated Java code snippet.
Identify potential ethical or legal concerns (e.g., bias, IP infringement).
Write a brief reflection on how these concerns could be addressed.

title: Lecture 9: Ethical, Legal, and Societal Implications of AI-Assisted Software Engineering
Lecture 9: Ethical, Legal, and Societal Implications of AI-Assisted Software Engineering
Lecture 9: Ethical, Legal, and Societal Implications of AI-Assisted Software Engineering
text:
Reflection Essay
Write a 500-word essay on ethical challenges in AI-assisted development. Consider bias, IP, accountability, and societal impact.

title: Lecture 9: Ethical, Legal, and Societal Implications of AI-Assisted Software Engineering
text:
Further Reading

GitHub Copilot documentation: Ethics & Security
Selected academic papers on AI ethics in software engineering

title: Lab 3: AI System Implementation
text:
Course Name/Number: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
Week: 7
Points: 100
This lab extends Lab 2: Eval-Driven Development. Continue maintaining the Weekly AI Interaction Log.

title: Lab 3: AI System Implementation
text:
1 Purpose of Assignment
This assignment is intended to help you:

Integrate components into a working coding agent workflow
Connect structured input, AI generation, and evaluation
Transition from isolated tasks to a cohesive system

title: Lab 3: AI System Implementation
text:
2 Steps / Requirements of Assignment
Integrate your structured input and evaluation work into a cohesive workflow. The system does not need to be fully autonomous, but it should show a repeatable path from task request to generated or revised code to evaluation.

Define the main workflow stages.
Connect the task specification to code generation or revision.
Connect the generated output to your evaluation layer.
Add clear instructions or a command that runs the workflow.
Document the architecture and current limitations.

title: Lab 3: AI System Implementation
text:
3 Action Items

Build the system in your repository.
Ensure the system is runnable.
Document the architecture.
Record prompts, iterations, and evaluation outcomes in your weekly log.

title: Lab 3: AI System Implementation
text:
4 Deliverables
Your repository should include:

Working source code for the integrated workflow.
Architecture documentation in the README or a separate design document.
A sample task input and resulting output.
Evaluation results from at least one workflow run.

title: Lab 3: AI System Implementation
text:
5 Grading







Category
Points




Workflow integrates structured input, generation, and evaluation
35


System is runnable and produces inspectable output
25


Architecture documentation is clear
20


Sample run and evaluation results are included
15


AI interaction evidence is included
5


Total
100

title: Lab 3: AI System Implementation
text:

title: Lab 2: Eval-Driven Development
text:
Course Name/Number: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
Week: 5
Points: 100
This lab extends Lab 1: Structured AI Workflow. Continue maintaining the Weekly AI Interaction Log.

title: Lab 2: Eval-Driven Development
text:
1 Purpose of Assignment
This assignment is intended to help you:

Build the evaluation layer of your coding agent
Define measurable correctness criteria
Detect regressions in AI-generated outputs

title: Lab 2: Eval-Driven Development
text:
2 Steps / Requirements of Assignment
Extend your Lab 1 repository by adding an evaluation system for generated or revised code. The evaluation layer should make correctness criteria explicit and repeatable.

Identify the feature or task your evaluation will check.
Define measurable acceptance criteria.
Add automated checks, tests, scripts, or structured review criteria.
Run the evaluation against at least two versions or iterations of the code.
Document what the evaluation caught and what it did not catch.

title: Lab 2: Eval-Driven Development
text:
3 Action Items

Extend your existing repository.
Add an evaluation system.
Document how to run evaluations.
Record Copilot interactions and evaluation decisions in your weekly log.

title: Lab 2: Eval-Driven Development
text:
4 Deliverables
Your repository should include:

Evaluation criteria in a documented format.
Runnable evaluation code, tests, scripts, or checklist.
Example evaluation results.
README instructions for running the evaluations from a clean environment.

title: Lab 2: Eval-Driven Development
text:

Category
Points




Evaluation criteria are measurable and tied to the task
25


Evaluation system is implemented and runnable
35


Regression or iteration comparison is documented
20


README explains how to run and interpret evaluations
15


AI interaction evidence is included
5


Total
100

title: Lab 2: Eval-Driven Development
text:

Submit your repository link and any required evaluation output through Canvas.

title: Weekly AI Interaction Log
text:
Course Name/Number: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
Weeks: 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10

title: Weekly AI Interaction Log
text:
1 Purpose of Assignment
This assignment is intended to help you learn to do the following:

Develop intentional and structured use of GitHub Copilot
Reflect on how prompting, iteration, and evaluation affect outcomes
Identify patterns in successful and unsuccessful AI interactions
Build awareness of your own engineering decision-making process
Document how AI-assisted development evolves over time

title: Weekly AI Interaction Log
text:
2 Steps / Requirements of Assignment
Maintain a running AI interaction log from Week 2 through Week 10. A single document, a folder of weekly Markdown files, or a dedicated section in your course repository is acceptable.
Each weekly entry should include:

Week number and date
Development task or learning activity
Your intent before using Copilot
Prompt, comment, or interaction summary
Copilot output or suggestion summary
What you accepted, modified, or rejected
How you evaluated the result
Reflection on what worked, what failed, and what you will change next time

title: Weekly AI Interaction Log
text:
3 Action Items

Create a running AI interaction log in your repository.
Add one entry each week from Week 2 through Week 10.
Record enough detail that another reader can understand your intent, the AI interaction, and your engineering decision.
Submit the weekly entry according to your professor’s Canvas instructions.

title: Weekly AI Interaction Log
text:

title: Final Project Milestone 2: Partial Implementation, Evaluation Strategy, and Identified Risks
text:
2 Steps / Requirements of Assignment
Submit a partial implementation that demonstrates the core direction of your final project. The system does not need to be complete, but it must show meaningful progress and a concrete evaluation strategy.

Implement the most important workflow path or system component.
Add or refine the evaluation strategy.
Run the current system on at least one example task.
Identify current risks, limitations, and incomplete pieces.
Update your plan for the final submission.

title: Lab 1: Structured AI Workflow
text:
Course Name/Number: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
Week: 3
Points: 90
Continue maintaining the Weekly AI Interaction Log while completing this lab.

title: Lab 1: Structured AI Workflow
text:
1 Purpose of Assignment
This assignment is intended to help you:

Establish the task input layer of your coding agent
Translate feature requests into structured specifications
Compare naive vs structured AI-assisted development
Begin building the foundation of your agent system

title: Lab 1: Structured AI Workflow
text:
2 Steps / Requirements of Assignment
Build the first layer of a Controlled Coding Agent workflow: a structured task input process. Use GitHub Copilot intentionally, but keep human judgment visible in your documentation.

Choose a small Java feature request that can be implemented in a short program or utility class.
Implement the feature once using a naive prompt or informal comment.
Create a structured task specification that includes goal, inputs, outputs, constraints, edge cases, and acceptance criteria.
Re-implement or revise the feature using the structured specification.
Compare the two approaches in your README.

title: Lab 1: Structured AI Workflow
text:
3 Action Items

Create a Git repository or use your continuing course repository.
Implement both the naive and structured approaches.
Include documentation in README.md.
Record relevant Copilot interactions in your weekly AI interaction log.

title: Lab 1: Structured AI Workflow
text:
4 Deliverables
Your repository should include:

Java source code for both approaches or clearly separated commits showing each approach.
A structured task specification.
A README.md explaining how to run the code and comparing naive vs structured development.
Evidence of AI-assisted development through prompts, comments, screenshots, or log links.

title: Lab 1: Structured AI Workflow
text:
5 Grading







Category
Points




Structured task specification is clear and complete
25

25

20

15

5


Total
90

text:

title: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering Orientation
text: Quick Links: - Syllabus & Weekly Schedule - Tool Setup Guide

title: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering Orientation
text:
1) Welcome & Course Introduction
Instructor: Add name, office hours, contact preferences
Format: 12-week · 4 credits · Prerequisite: COMP 311 | Advanced Java Programming (or equivalent)
Why this course now?
AI-assisted development is changing how software is built. You’ll learn to pair your expertise with tools like GitHub Copilot to generate code, tests, docs, and plans—while critically evaluating quality, security, and ethics.
Course arc (high-level):

Week 1: Environment setup and AI-assisted software engineering foundations
Weeks 2–10: Weekly AI Interaction Log documenting prompts, decisions, and evaluations
Weeks 3, 5, 7, 9, and 10: Labs that build a Controlled Coding Agent workflow
Weeks 10–12: Final Project proposal, partial implementation, evaluation strategy, risk analysis, final system, and demo

title: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering Orientation
text:
2) Syllabus & Learning Outcomes
See the Syllabus & Weekly Schedule for full course description, learning outcomes, topics, grading breakdown, and weekly activities.

Catalog Description
Explores how software engineers can use AI-assisted tools intentionally while building controlled, evaluable coding-agent workflows. Emphasis is placed on structured task input, AI-assisted code generation, evaluation, risk analysis, human oversight, reproducibility, and professional documentation. Hands-on labs build toward a final Controlled Coding Agent project in Java.
Learning Outcomes (mapped to Bloom’s levels):

Set up and verify a Java development environment with GitHub Copilot and GitHub-based submission workflows. (Apply)
Document AI-assisted development decisions through weekly interaction logs. (Analyze)
Translate feature requests into structured task specifications for AI-assisted implementation. (Create)
Define and run evaluations that measure correctness and detect regressions in AI-generated outputs. (Evaluate)
Integrate structured input, AI-assisted generation, evaluation, and human approval into a controlled coding-agent workflow. (Create)
Analyze reliability, security, maintainability, and production-readiness risks in AI-assisted systems. (Analyze/Evaluate)
Deliver a complete Controlled Coding Agent final project with documentation, evaluation evidence, and a reproducible demo. (Create)

title: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering Orientation
text:
3) Tools & Setup (Week 1 Assignment)
See the Tool Setup Guide for step-by-step instructions for installing and configuring your IDE, Java, GitHub Copilot, and related tools.

Required Software/Accounts - JDK (latest LTS) - IntelliJ IDEA or Visual Studio Code - Git + GitHub account - GitHub Copilot with IDE plugin; students should set up GitHub Education using their university email address at github.com/settings/education/benefits - Maven, Gradle, and JUnit 5 are introduced later for labs and evaluations
Setup Checklist - [ ] Install JDK and confirm java -version - [ ] Install IntelliJ or VS Code - [ ] Install Git and configure SSH/HTTPS for GitHub - [ ] Set up GitHub Education with your university email address - [ ] Enable GitHub Copilot and sign in from IDE - [ ] Create a sample Java project; generate a simple method using Copilot - [ ] Confirm a simple Java project runs
Submission (Assignment 1): - Screenshots of IDE + Copilot in action - Brief reflection (150–250 words) on setup experience and Week 1 concepts

title: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering Orientation
text:
4) Course Logistics
See the Syllabus & Weekly Schedule for details on modality, workload, assessment weights, deadlines, and submission conventions.

Modality & Rhythm - Weekly cadence: lecture/discussion (concepts), live demo, guided lab, short reflection - Estimated workload: 8–10 hours/week
Assessments & Weights - Assignment 1: Environment Setup & AI-Assisted SWE Foundations: 30 points - Weekly AI Interaction Log: 90 points - Labs 1–5: 480 points total, building the Controlled Coding Agent workflow over time - Final Project Milestones and Final Submission: 400 points total - Total: 1000 points
Deadlines & Late Policy - Standard deadline: end of week (Sunday 11:59 PM local) - Extensions for documented circumstances—contact instructor early per university policies
Submission Conventions - Code via GitHub; submit Canvas link, repository link, and commit hash when requested - Unless your professor instructs otherwise, continue extending the same project repository through Labs 1–5 and the Final Project - Include README.md with how to run, how to evaluate, and AI usage notes - Maintain weekly AI interaction log entries from Week 2 through Week 10 - Final submissions must include reproducible setup/run instructions and a demo link when required

title: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering Orientation
section: 5) AI Use, Evidence, and Academic Integrity
text:
5) AI Use, Evidence, and Academic Integrity
GitHub Copilot is the approved AI assistant for course programming work unless an assignment says otherwise. You may use Copilot to help draft code, tests, documentation, evaluation criteria, and workflow artifacts, but you remain responsible for every submitted artifact.
Required Evidence

Record meaningful Copilot interactions in the Weekly AI Interaction Log.
Describe AI assistance in each repository README or assignment documentation.
Preserve prompts, accepted or rejected suggestions, manual edits, evaluations, and final decisions when relevant.

Not Allowed

Submitting AI-generated work without acknowledgment.
Submitting code you do not understand or cannot explain.
Sharing full solutions with classmates unless the professor explicitly makes an activity collaborative.
Pasting secrets, private data, proprietary code, or protected information into AI tools.

title: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering Orientation
section: 6) Risks To Watch Throughout the Course
text:
6) Risks To Watch Throughout the Course
The labs and Final Project ask you to evaluate AI-assisted systems, not just produce code. Keep these risks visible in your weekly logs, evaluations, and project documentation.

Correctness: Generated code may appear plausible while missing edge cases or requirements.
Security: AI suggestions may introduce unsafe input handling, secret leakage, weak cryptography, or injection risks.
Maintainability: Generated code may be hard to read, overcomplicated, or poorly aligned with your architecture.
Evaluation gaps: Passing one demo does not prove the workflow is reliable. Define measurable checks.
Human oversight: Controlled workflows need clear points where a human reviews, approves, rejects, or revises AI output.

title: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering Orientation
text:
7) Learning Activities
Controlled Workflow Pattern

Specify: Translate a request into structured input with goals, constraints, edge cases, and acceptance criteria
Generate or revise: Use Copilot to propose implementation, tests, documentation, or workflow artifacts
Evaluate: Run tests, scripts, checks, or structured reviews against explicit criteria
Control: Add human approval checkpoints before accepting important changes
Document: Preserve prompts, outputs, evaluation results, risks, and final decisions

This pattern appears repeatedly in Labs 1-5 and the Final Project. The goal is not to maximize AI usage; the goal is to make AI-assisted development controlled, observable, evaluable, and reproducible.

title: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering Orientation
text:
8) Success Strategies

Keep the Weekly AI Interaction Log current: record prompts, outputs, evaluations, decisions, and what worked
Prefer small, precise prompts over broad requests; iterate quickly
Define evaluation criteria before trusting generated code; use AI to scaffold edge cases where appropriate
Use tests, static analysis, security checks, and manual review alongside AI suggestions
Commit early/often; preserve artifacts that make workflow decisions reproducible
Treat AI like a junior pair-programmer: useful for drafts, but never the final authority

title: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering Orientation
text:
9) Orientation Activities (Week 1)
Icebreaker (Forum): Share your first experience using AI on codewhat surprised you?
Mini-Demo (in class or video): Instructor demonstrates Copilot in a small Java file, then critiques the output for intent match, correctness, and risk.
Quick Poll: Which part of a controlled AI workflow seems most challenging? Options: specifying tasks, generating code, evaluating outputs, managing risk, documenting decisions
Checklist TurnIn: Confirm tools installed; Copilot active; simple Java project running.

title: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering Orientation
text:
10) Week 1 Deliverables

Assignment 1: Setup verification + short reflection
Weekly AI Interaction Log: Begins in Week 2 and continues through Week 10

Rubric Snapshot (Assignment 1)

Environment, GitHub, and Copilot setup completed — 10 points
Screenshots clearly demonstrate setup — 5 points
Repository includes minimal Java project and README — 5 points
Reflection demonstrates understanding of Week 1 concepts — 10 points

title: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering Orientation
text:

Course site / Canvas module: link

Contact Protocol

For bugs or repo issues — open a GitHub Issue
For grading/logistics — course forum or email
For private matters — office hours or direct message

title: Syllabus: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
text:
This syllabus is referenced in the Course Orientation and provides the full course structure, learning outcomes, topics, grading, and weekly schedule. For tool installation and setup, see the Tool Setup Guide.
Course Name/Number: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
Format: 12-week
Credits: 4
Prerequisite: COMP 311 – Advanced Java Programming or equivalent programming experience

title: Syllabus: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
text:
Course Description
This course explores how software engineers can use AI-assisted tools intentionally while building controlled, evaluable coding-agent workflows. Students will use GitHub Copilot, Java, GitHub, testing tools, evaluation criteria, and structured documentation to move from basic AI-assisted coding toward a complete Controlled Coding Agent final project. Emphasis is placed on structured task input, eval-driven development, human oversight, risk analysis, reproducibility, and critically evaluating AI-generated outputs.

title: Syllabus: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
text:
Learning Outcomes
By the end of the course, students will be able to: - Set up and verify a Java development environment with GitHub Copilot and GitHub-based submission workflows. (Apply) - Document AI-assisted development decisions through weekly interaction logs. (Analyze) - Translate feature requests into structured task specifications for AI-assisted implementation. (Create) - Define and run evaluations that measure correctness and detect regressions in AI-generated outputs. (Evaluate) - Integrate structured input, AI-assisted generation, evaluation, and human approval into a controlled coding-agent workflow. (Create) - Analyze reliability, security, maintainability, and production-readiness risks in AI-assisted systems. (Analyze / Evaluate) - Deliver a complete Controlled Coding Agent final project with documentation, evaluation evidence, and a reproducible demo. (Create)

title: Syllabus: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
text:
Topics Covered

Environment setup and AI-assisted software engineering foundations
GitHub Copilot, GitHub Education, Git, Java IDEs, and repository-based submission workflows
Weekly AI interaction logging and evidence-based reflection
Structured AI workflows and task specifications
Eval-driven development, measurable correctness criteria, and regression detection
Integrated AI system implementation with structured input, generation, and evaluation
Risk analysis, reliability, security, maintainability, and production readiness
Controlled coding-agent workflows with human oversight checkpoints
Final Project proposal, architecture, partial implementation, evaluation strategy, complete system, and demo

title: Syllabus: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
text:
Textbooks & Materials

Selected academic journal and conference papers on AI in software engineering
Coursera specialization: Generative AI for Software Engineering
Official GitHub Copilot documentation and tutorials
Practical guides on prompt engineering and AI tooling focused on Copilot
Supplementary readings on evaluation, software testing, reproducibility, responsible AI, and software security

title: Syllabus: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
text:
Tools and Software
See the Tool Setup Guide for installation and configuration walkthroughs.

GitHub Copilot (primary AI tool integrated into Java IDEs). Students should set up GitHub Education with their university email address at github.com/settings/education/benefits to access education benefits, including Copilot access when approved.
Visual Studio Code or IntelliJ IDEA with GitHub Copilot plugin installed
Latest JDK
Git and GitHub for version control and project collaboration
JUnit 5 for Java unit testing
Maven or Gradle for build and dependency management
